speaker: interviewer
text: This is April 25, 1995. We are taping Solomon Kupitz. And this is tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the hospital, and they tried to help me everywhere they can. They always brought in, I saw, all kind of children, little children. And in the hospital, actually, the building where the hospital was a theater. Upstairs was the hospital and downstairs was-- was a regular theater going on. They were playing shows. Yiddish shows with all Romanian actors and actresses. And my bed was by the window. So I used to look down on it because I heard the piano playing, and I always like music. So at least I didn't have to think much about my pains. And there was also a once in awhile came in German, not SS, but from the German authorities to check on the hospital. And when they came in, I thought they came in to kill us all, but looked like that they did not have no authority to do that. And I saw a lot of things. When I got better-- and I could-- I couldn't pick up my legs and my hands-- but later on when I got better and Dr. Drobner, and Dr. Schwartz and Dr. Liul'kin, they would all go to me, and they were trying everything to do that I should survive. And I saw other children, I saw a little boy and a-- and a girl, the brother and sister, their father was a doctor. And they brought them in very, very sick from another town. And I saw they did not have nothing to eat. So when I felt better, I run out from the ghetto. And I got for them bread and milk and eggs. And I stole it, and I brought it. They should have something to eat. But they had very bad sickness. And the boy, I saw him dying. First, the sister, the little one died, and then I saw him and I was standing right near when they covered him up. And I look, and I wish that boy would survive. He was so sweet. He must have been maybe 10 years old. So there were others. And then when I got better, I got out from the hospital, I came back, and to-- always, Dr. Drobner tried to tell me was my heart was not good because of this terrible infection of arthritis. So they thought I had endocardiac, which is infected the tissues of the heart. But many doctors said, no, and some said yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 19-- that was already close to 1944, and the Red Army was already at [? Trada. ?] I think that Kiev was already liberated. And the Germans were running all the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people in the ghetto, they thought that maybe because the Germans and the Romanians will be close-- the Germans will closer in so the Romanians will not have no target if the Germans want to take over the ghetto and then they liquidate even this ghetto. So I know that Dr. [? Heshman ?] from Judenrat with his whole family and some of his girls that he had there run away to Mogilev. And this was on the border between Romania. And my brother, who came with me, and he thought maybe we should run away too more closer to Romania. Because if you go closer to Romania, maybe you have a chance to survive. So one night, me and my brother got into a train, which was with the coal loaded full. Some-- somehow we got inside. We closed it. And in the meantime, as we were waiting on the station, to the train should start to move, all of a sudden, the Russian two planes start to come in. And they start to bomb the station, right near as the bombs were falling. We didn't care no more. Better to fall-- to die from a bomb than the dead that I saw. And somehow, in the middle-- in the morning, the train start to move. And we went to Mogilev, which was a whole day, a whole night, almost half of a night. We came into Mogilev, and we were looking for Jews or the ghetto. And we came in, somebody told us that-- that Dr. [? Heshman's ?] hiding somewhere in-- in Mogilev.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I wanted to know. But when we came and they told us it's not there. And we did not have-- and we saw Germans all over, on the station, running already into Romania because the Russians were closing and closing closer. So we went back to the station. He wanted to go back to Zhmerinka. As we came in-- into the train, we-- we were in one of the wagons all alone by us, and then came in all of a sudden a Romanian conductor knocks into the door. And he comes in and he says, what are doing over here? You are Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we said, yes. We're going back to-- he wanted to arrest us. So he asked us if we have gold or we have something else. Somehow I had a leather, a skin of leather in my-- in my-- all around. And we had breads and we had other things and we gave it him. And he let us stay. He didn't do nothing to us. And we can back to Zhmerinka with the train. As we came back to Zhmerinka, the people over there, they were already talking, everybody was talking that the Russians already-- this was already 1944. In the beginning, like January, February, and the Red Army already took, I think, Kiev. And they're closing in, closer and closer on Vinnitsa. And by the way, in 1943, I didn't mention that, Hitler came to Vinnitsa to give orders. When [? Stalingrad-- ?] in 1940, when [? Stalingrad ?] was formed, his headquarters was in Vinnitsa. And because he came to Vinnitsa, they killed in Vinnitsa 30,000 to 40,000 Jews just before he came also in graves, in the out-- outskirts of Vinnitsa, which was a couple of graves and also in Odessa. In Odessa was killed maybe 50,000 or 60,000. In Zhytomyr, I was Zhytomyr, was killed about 30,000 and Berdychiv. In the Ukraine was killed millions, maybe a million and a half, maybe more. But they never mentioned actually so much were the Jews in Ukraine were all murdered that they mention so much about concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about that the rest of the Jews? From the Russian Jews, Romanian Jews, which did not go to concentration but in the ghettos and so on and so on. So there were graves. There are graves in every town, in every city, from-- from the Polish border up to Kharkiv, up to Kiev, all over into [? Smudens, ?] Jewish graves in the Ukraine, which they don't even know where the graves because the Russians built buildings on these graves. So when I-- when we were in Zhmerinka, we knew already something is going to happen. The Russians are closing. And so I thought I would run away again because I don't want to be in the ghetto because if we are-- we saw the Germans already with trucks coming in , coming in. So I took a-- a girl, she was my age, and her parents said, you know what? You go away from the ghetto, another town, which was another 10 kilometers. And that town they call it Stanislavchik.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as it was still cold, there was snow over there, and it's still cold until May. It was in March. And I got dressed, and she got dressed like a little Christian girl. And she took a-- a chicken with her, a live chicken, and I took some eggs, [INAUDIBLE]. So we knew if-- if on the road the Germans going to catch us, we're gonna give them this, maybe they're not gonna shoot us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as we were going out from the city of Zhmerinka, we going with the railroad tracks into that other town, snow. And as we come out from the town, I see already Germans with a [? rig ?] over their helms in battle. In front I look, I see artillery, antiaircraft artillery, all kind of artillery, already digged in, waiting for the Russians to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I walked with her running, we were walking actually, and I see these German. And this was a feldgendarmerie with a big black steal over here. And he says, halt, stop, as we come near. And we stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN], what do you have over here? So she says I have a kuritsa, I have a chicken. [SPEAKING GERMAN]? I said I have eggs, ei-- eier, eggs. So he said, OK, give it to me. And she gave him this. And he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Get out, [GERMAN]. So he told to go straight.

speaker: interviewee
text: As we were running straight, we saw Germans all over in trenches already. We were lucky that they just let us-- they didn't shoot at us. And we came in the late, late in the evening. We came to that town. As we came to the town, we didn't see nobody. Bombed out little houses. So we walked in a little house, no windows, was cold freezing, and it's night. And I was wearing a jacket. And she was wearing that coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was shivering. So she laid down on the floor, tried to sleep. And I didn't want to sleep because I thought, what's gonna happen? So I took off my jacket, and I thought she's gonna be cold. And I put in on her. And I was standing just in a little shirt.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, I hear shooting. It was coming already, light. And all of a sudden, I look out, there was a mountain, and Russian partisans, the first patrol are running down from the hill, coming down into the street. As I saw this, I said to her, here is the Russians are coming, this [INAUDIBLE] patrol. I run down to the street, and I see this Russian and a horse. He jumps down with his machine guns. And I told him, we are-- so why-- we-- we just came here to-- to say we are Jewish. And he grabbed me around. He start-- and we start to kiss him. And he said, right this moment, you come-- come around the [INAUDIBLE]. Don't go back over there because there are Germans all around. And as I were talking to him, I see already shooting at the-- at one of the Germans in the slack. And they got already prisoners, German prisoners. They were hiding Germans. They came up with their hands. And many more Russians came out, not too many Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was so nice. Then I asked him, for where are you coming from? He says, I'm coming from Stalingrad up to here. He must have been maybe 19, 20, something, not even 20. And we were very happy. And we told him we are from Zhmerinka. And he says, you don't go back to Zhmerinka now.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have-- you'll be able to go back maybe tonight or tomorrow because Zhmerinka's circled, and the SS is fighting in Zhmerinka. And at night we saw already fire all over Zhmerinka. And he said he has to go because he's on only on a patrol. And he said goodbye to us. And he went. As he went back on the road to fight, because he was always in a patrol, and the way between Stanislav between the town of Zhmerinka, he was shot by a German artillery or something. [INAUDIBLE] two days later, as me this girl-- her name was Fira Kogan. She's in Israel. As we were going back to Zhmerinka, I saw him laying, his horse on one side and he like on the side of the road was dead. I couldn't believe it. He was coming from Stalingrad and fighting, and here he is dead. When he came to Zhmerinka, the Red Army was over there already. It was already liberated. And I went into the house. I saw my sister, my brother-in-law, my brother, and some of my friends. Nobody was killed in Zhmerinka, except some Ukrainians who were fighting against the Russians. There were [? shadows ?] and somebody is lying in the back of the house. We were-- they were all Russians, Ukrainians, and many Germans. As I came in the ghetto, it was no more ghetto. And everybody was happy see the Russians. And I walked in in one of the places in the-- in what used to be the ghetto to get some bread, whatever. And there was a lot of wood in there, and I saw SS men, maybe eight or 10 of them all dead, shot with their black uniforms, laying by-- under the wood. And we were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my friend said, let's take off the-- the clothing from them. Let's-- let's-- they're not allowed-- they not even deserved to be laying over there. And then Zhmerinka was liberated. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944 in March, March maybe 27. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after that, yeah, my-- the people start to get organized. Said-- they came in-- the commander of the Russian headquarters was Jewish. And he had a son. He was like my age. Just before there were Russian partisans, and they could-- before, actually a couple weeks before, and my friend of mine was in the partisan. And he asked me, had I gone with him, if I want to go out closer to do the same what he was doing. Actually, I wanted but I went away over there. Then we-- we were still in that little town, Stanislavchik. The Red Army when they came in, they wanted me and her to go with them, with the Red Army, further out and to get eventually into the army.  They took children in the army. But we were carrying ammunition on our backs-- and it was very, very happy for-- heavy-- for a couple days. But I felt it's too hard for me. And I went back. I said, let's get out, in the middle of the night, and let's go back to Zhmerinka. So in Zhmerinka, the headquarters of the police, the military, [? militia, ?] and the-- they where establishing order. And there were-- there were many Jewish partisans. And first what they start to do is to catch collaborators and murderers who killed the people, the Jewish people, and my family in Brailov. A couple weeks later, they captured one of the murderers, Kobaliuk. And a matter of fact, I was going there too, me, my brother, my older brother, we went to Brailov. As we came to Brailov, we ask-- the Ukrainians were afraid to come close to us because they're afraid that we gonna kill them. And we didn't do anything. We didn't stay long. But we got that Kobaliuk. We-- on the way-- on the way back to Zhmerinka, the Russian army was going all the time with their tanks. And they-- they saw we are having somebody with-- with a gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got him, he was hiding, through other Ukrainians. They told us where he's hiding. And there were a couple of us. And we got in, and we-- we got him. Some of the Russian soldiers said, shoot him. Kill him right away. Where are taking them? He doesn't deserve to be taken back to headquarters. Kill him. But we didn't want to kill him right away. And we took him back to the headquarters in Zhmerinka. In Zhmerinka, they made a tribunal. A week later, they-- he was hanged. He was shot or hanged. He died in-- in Zhmerinka. And there were others also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- then they were looking later on for Dr. [? Heshman ?] because he was also actually collaborating. He did bad things. And they got him too. Him, my brother-in-law got him with others. And there was a trial.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, the trial was in Vinnitsa. And they gave him, I don't know if they give death, or-- but they gave him prison for the rest of his life. What I heard, he died in prison later on. So then in 19-- the end of already '44 came people from their location from deep Russia and came a medical mission from Moscow to see how the Jewish people were killed in the Ukraine. They came to the grave. And they digged out bodies. And they ask me questions. And I told them all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were even writers. There was one very famous poet, a Russian Jewish poet, who came with them. And he also-- he mentioned how he couldn't understand that so many Jews went to die without resistance. For me, it was very easy to understand. Where was the resistance? From the Ukrainians? If they-- if the Germans did not kill you, the Ukrainians would murder you left and right, which they did. So for what I couldn't-- was there any leaders to organize us? Did they came? Nobody. So I would just listening and thinking when he said to him. It was easier because he came deep in the Soviet Union where the army were fighting, where it was trained.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not trained. We were trained to only to go and get killed. So then I saw between them from the Red Cross of Switzerland was Englishmen. And I saw one English diplomat. He must have been a diplomat. And I asked him if he speaks Russian. He says yes. And I told him I have a father in America. And I would like to give you just his name and his address. I remembered his address. And I gave him, and he said, I'll do that. And he took the address. And when he came back to England or wherever, within three months later, I received a letter from my father in Zhmerinka. And my father wanted to know what happened to the whole family. And I wrote a big letter, everything. That was the end of '44, like in August, July/August. I received right away packages from my father, food packages, all kind of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with your one brother and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- my brother-- my older brother was taken to the Red Army. My older-- the one who survived. And he was with the Red Army in [? Zhutomyr ?] first. And then he went to Konigsberg, where the second front line from the [? White ?] Russian front of fighting in [? Pruse, ?] in Prussia. And he was in antitank artillery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with him, I couldn't-- he wrote letters all the time from the army. I was with him when he still stayed in the Ukraine. They took him right after the liberation. And-- Your sister and brother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was my-- with my brother-in-law. He was a tailor. They opened up a factory to makes suits and coats for the people who worked at the railroad. They had uniforms. And the people who worked at the railroad, they had a certain [? allot ?] from the Red Army. They don't have to go into the army so they can have-- because this is what they were doing was just as good as they were in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother-in-law organized a big factory with a lot of people working. And the fabrics was given by the government. The fabrics came from the United States, from England, and they made uniforms for the railroads. And I used to help them out too. So I had a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- you had to have a job already by the Russians and to be reported by the KGB every day, every week. They give you a black book that if you do anything bad or something, you are responsible what you're doing. So every turn, they have an eye on you. But then my father wrote already a letter that he send out affidavits for me to come to the United States with the State Department. And they sent to Moscow to the American Embassy. That was already 1945. In 1945, in May-- no, in July, the end of June/July, I-- yeah, I wrote to the-- the American Embassy wrote me  a letter, which I have it over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: American Embassy where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Moscow. And the ambassador was Averell Harriman. And he wrote me a letter that I have a visa from the State Department to come to the United States. But because I'm a Soviet citizen, but I was born in Poland, I have to write to demand from the Polish Embassy in Moscow to get me a Polish passport and a Soviet exit visa, and then I will get the American visa. But to get all of this, you as a Russian citizen, you have to get permission by the Russian government to come to Moscow. But we sent you an invitation. And that's all. I was-- I knew right away. I went to Russian lawyers to find out what I can do with this. They tell me forget about it. Don't do anything because you'll be sent to Siberia. I didn't stop. I went to the KGB in Vinnitsa. There was the headquarters. And I came in in the office of the international, [? avere ?] they call it, international branch of the KGB. There was a girl sitting there. She was Jewish. And I told her what I need, and I told her what I want and where I'm going and that I want to see my father. She got scared. She said, I will help you every way I can. I have a friend in Moscow. When you go to Moscow, I'll give you a letter to her. You can stay by her. But that's all what I can do it. But a [? propiska, ?] a special permit by the KGB, you must get it from them or directly from Moscow, which was an impossibility. I still didn't stop. I went to Kiev. Kiev was the capital city of the Ukraine. And I knew that this maybe as a Ukrainian and Russian citizen, I'll be able to do something in Kiev. First, when I came to Kiev, I send that telegram to the American Embassy. And I said that because I'm a Russian citizen, I cannot get a Soviet exit visa. And I cannot get a Polish passport. If there's a possibility if I can get an American passport, then I will get a Soviet exit visa. The American Embassy answered me right away. Because you're not an American born, you cannot get American passport. But we invite you to come to Moscow. You'll try to get a Polish passport. And then when you have the Polish passport, you get the Soviet exit visa. This made me a little bit feeling better. I came back to Zhmerinka. I knew the head of the KGB, railroad KGB, not the regular civilian, and they I went to the chief. And I told them the story. But he was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, you know what? I'm gonna give you a permit to go to Moscow to buy all kinds of things that we need for the railroad. Maybe this will help you. And he gave it to me. Also I had the letter from the American Embassy. I had also a letter from the Polish [? patriots-- ?] [? patriots ?] were the Polish organization in Moscow-- that I am Polish, which helped me too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And go to Moscow, then you'll have to get a permit to get into a train, which was impossible. Only Russian generals, officers, and soldiers were right to go to Moscow. But somehow I knew the [? nanchanik ?] of the station. And he gave me a ticket to go to Moscow. He did not know what he was doing. But he gave it to me. And I came to the train at night, and I was wearing a Russian uniform, not a military but the uniform from the railroad, which was instead of green was blue. And I came into the train, my sister took me there and my brother-in-law. And I took food with me because I knew in Moscow there was nothing to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had with me bread. I also took some leather because I thought maybe I'll give some a piece a bigger, a skin of leather, I'll get something for that. And I had other things and apples and fruits. And I put in whatever I could in between my jacket. On the train, soldiers we stopped on the way to Kiev. It took us maybe two, three days. To get to Moscow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the train was so slow. To get to Moscow. Soldiers were coming from everywhere. They couldn't get into the trains because there was nothing or-- because soldiers, Russians, were coming from already East Germany, from Berlin, they wanted to go home. So there was no-- nobody to be able to get in the civilians. But because I was wearing a uniform, that means I'm a worker of the railroad, I had a chance even to tell the others that you cannot come in. So-- somehow-- I was in the train going to Moscow. It took almost five days. As we coming into Moscow, they told us that before you come in the station, this is-- Moscow had a lot of stations, metros, subway stations. And I came in into the Kiev station.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told us that you have to take out all the documents. You must have a special permit from the KGB in Moscow to be able to go into the city, otherwise they'll arrest you. And the way as I come in as I was in the train, another Jewish guy from Lithuania. So he came in, I don't know how, and he said where are going? So I tell him I'm going to Moscow. Do you have a [? propiska? ?] I said, no. Do you have? He says no. But what do you have? I got bread I got the apples where you go, I'll go. So I said, what happens if they arrest? So I'm gonna wait for you. We come in already to the stop in Moscow. And we go down from the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see two policemen standing at the gate, one in one side. And there were many people, farmers, bringing in fruits and vegetables into the city. So they were checked. So I-- so I was a little boy. I was not-- I was short. I saw one is checked. One is checked, one policeman, and the other one checks the other one. I just walk through. And nobody stopped me. And I-- and he supposed to wait for me in the station. Said, oh, he's there. I'll wait for him. I come down and he's already there. And that was the subway, the metro. And then we went further. And that's what happened.
